title: Carved sycamore tea-caddy spoon
subtitle: Small treen spoon for loose-leaf tea and coffee
excerpt: A tea-caddy spoon: 10 cm overall, with a deep round bowl that holds a level 2 g of loose-leaf tea. Sycamore is the classic treen wood; it dries food-safe pale and takes no flavour. The project introduces the thumb-pivot cut as the primary shaping move on a very small workpiece.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885); tea-caddy spoons as a standard treen form are documented in Victoria and Albert Museum collection records.

glossaryTerms.definition: Small household objects turned or carved from wood; spoons, scoops, cups, and kitchen implements of the pre-industrial home.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
Blade pivoted by the off-hand thumb pressing on the back of the blade; short controlled cuts for fine shaping on a small workpiece.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, cutting off the end of the workpiece into open air.

content.text: The tea-caddy spoon is
treen

at its most refined: 10 cm overall, a handle that fits two fingers, and a deep round bowl that holds exactly enough loose-leaf tea for one pot. Sycamore dries food-safe pale and adds no flavour to the tea leaves; it is the correct wood here. The project introduces the
thumb-pivot cut
as the main shaping move; at this small scale, the thumb-pivot gives more control than the full push cut.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest.
Sharpening: strop every fifteen minutes of carving; a dull edge on a small workpiece produces tears not shavings.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
A small green sycamore
blank

: 13 cm long, 4 cm wide, 2.5 cm deep. Sycamore grows commonly across the UK and is widely available as pruning waste. The blank is small enough to come from a branch section 5 cm in diameter split in half; no froe is needed. Lime or birch are good alternatives.
Method
Draw the profile: handle 7 cm, neck 1 cm, bowl round 2.5 cm diameter. The round bowl distinguishes this from an elongated eating-spoon bowl.
Shape the handle with
push cuts
along its length and
thumb-pivot cuts
around the neck and bowl transition. At this size the thumb-pivot gives finer control than a longer push cut.

Hollow the bowl with the small hook knife. Target depth: 1 cm. The bowl should hold a level volume of 2 ml, approximately. Work in short strokes from rim to centre; the small radius of the bowl means the hook knife arc is tight and only the inner curve of the blade is cutting.
Check wall thickness by holding to the light. The walls of a tea-caddy spoon bowl should be 2 mm; just enough to be rigid when the spoon is pressed into loose-leaf tea.
Leave to dry 1 week. At this small mass, sycamore dries quickly and without cracking if the walls are even.
Finishing
Sand with 180 then 220 grit for a smooth final surface; the small contact area of this spoon shows sanding scratches more than a larger piece. Apply raw linseed oil; 2-week cure before use with food.
Variations
Coffee caddy spoon: a smaller oval bowl, 1.8 cm across, matching a standard coffee-scoop volume.
Handle with a decorative end: the handle tip can be whittled to a small finial or spade shape.
Care
Wipe clean after each use with a damp cloth; no need to wash unless the spoon contacts wet food. Re-oil twice a year.

items.symptom: Bowl too shallow even after several hook-knife passes
items.cause: Small hook knife not reaching the centre of the bowl; the curvature of the blade is wider than the bowl radius
items.fix: Use only the innermost part of the blade curve on each stroke. A tight small hook knife is essential for the tea-caddy spoon bowl; the medium hook knife cannot reach the bowl centre at this size.

items.cause: Neck carved too narrow before the bowl was hollowed; hook-knife leverage cracked it
items.fix: Hollow the bowl before refining the neck. The neck should remain oversize until the bowl is complete.
